content.text: Reliability and Availability in Cloud Computing

Reliability and availability are two of the most important properties of any production system. A reliable system does what it is supposed to do — it computes correctly, stores data without corruption, and enforces the expected behaviour. An available system is one that can be reached and used at a given moment. The two properties are related but distinct: a system can be highly available (always reachable) while being unreliable (returning wrong answers), and it can be reliable (correct when functioning) while having low availability (frequently offline for maintenance).
the probability that a system performs its intended function correctly for a specified period under stated conditions
the proportion of time a system is in a functioning, reachable state — expressed as a percentage of total time
the probability that stored data survives without loss or corruption — a storage-specific dimension (S3's 99.999999999% durability means 11 nines)
the ability of a system to continue operating correctly even when individual components fail

attrs.alt: AWS Reliability and Availability Architecture showing AZs, load balancer, auto scaling, and the availability nines table
attrs.title: AWS Multi-Layer Reliability Architecture

content.text: Availability is measured as a percentage of uptime over a year. Each additional nine reduces the allowable downtime by roughly a factor of ten. The practical difference between 99.9% and 99.99% is the difference between eight hours and fifty-three minutes of downtime per year — enough to make an e-commerce site lose a day's revenue versus losing a single lunch-hour trade window.

Moving from 3 nines to 4 nines is not just a configuration change — it requires architectural changes: multi-AZ deployments, active health checks, automated failover, and rigorously tested recovery procedures. Each additional nine typically increases both complexity and cost.
Key Reliability Metrics: MTBF and MTTR
Availability can be computed directly from two operational metrics that every operations team should track:
the average time a system runs between failures. A higher MTBF means fewer failures.
the average time to restore service after a failure. A lower MTTR means faster recovery.
. A system that fails once per month (MTBF = 730 hours) and takes 30 minutes to recover (MTTR = 0.5 hours) has availability of 730 / 730.5 ≈ 99.93%.

Cloud services improve both metrics compared to on-premises: hardware is replaced faster (improving MTBF through better physical maintenance), and automated recovery tools such as EC2 Auto Recovery and RDS Multi-AZ failover dramatically reduce MTTR.

AWS takes responsibility for reliability at the physical and infrastructure layer under the Shared Responsibility Model. This includes hardware redundancy, network redundancy, power redundancy in data centres, and the global network backbone. Customers are responsible for designing their applications to use these facilities correctly.

Each AWS Region contains two or more Availability Zones (AZs). Each AZ is one or more discrete data centres with independent power, cooling, and physical security, separated from the others by meaningful geographic distance (typically 10–100 km) to prevent correlated failures from natural disasters, power grid failures, or cable cuts. The AZs within a region are connected by a low-latency, high-bandwidth private network, making synchronous replication practical at sub-10ms round-trip latency.
A flood that takes out the power to AZ-a does not affect AZ-b or AZ-c
A software deployment bug that crashes instances in one AZ still leaves healthy instances serving traffic in the others
Each AZ has its own independent network connections to the internet and to AWS services, preventing cascading failure

AWS Regions are geographically distant and fully independent. A region-level failure (rare, but happened with us-east-1 in 2011 and 2012) affects only customers who did not have a multi-region architecture. Services like Route 53 and CloudFront span regions and can route traffic away from an impaired region automatically. For workloads requiring very high availability, a pilot light or warm standby architecture in a second region provides region-level resilience.

Distributing application components across multiple AZs is the standard pattern for achieving 4–5 nines on AWS. The following example deploys an EC2 Auto Scaling group across two AZs behind an Application Load Balancer:

RDS Multi-AZ keeps a synchronous standby replica in a second AZ. If the primary instance fails, AWS automatically updates the DNS endpoint to point to the standby — the application reconnects to the same endpoint and resumes within 60 seconds, with no data loss (zero RPO — Recovery Point Objective). The failover is fully automated; no human intervention is required.

# Create a Multi-AZ RDS instance
aws rds create-db-instance 
  --db-instance-identifier my-prod-db 
  --db-instance-class db.t3.medium 
  --engine postgres 
  --master-username admin 
  --master-user-password 'SecurePass123!' 
  --allocated-storage 100 
  --multi-az 
  --storage-type gp3 
  --backup-retention-period 7

# Describe the failover status
aws rds describe-db-instances 
  --db-instance-identifier my-prod-db 
  --query 'DBInstances[0].{MultiAZ:MultiAZ,SecondaryAZ:SecondaryAvailabilityZone,Status:DBInstanceStatus}'

Automated recovery is what turns reliability engineering from a manual emergency response procedure into a system property. AWS provides several recovery primitives that operate without human involvement:
HTTP, HTTPS, or TCP health check

# Enable EC2 Auto Recovery via CloudWatch alarm
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm 
  --alarm-name ec2-auto-recover-i-0abc123 
  --namespace AWS/EC2 
  --metric-name StatusCheckFailed_System 
  --dimensions Name=InstanceId,Value=i-0abc123 
  --period 60 
  --evaluation-periods 2 
  --threshold 1 
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold 
  --statistic Maximum 
  --alarm-actions arn:aws:automate:us-east-1:ec2:recover
Two terms define the acceptable boundaries for a disaster recovery design:
the maximum acceptable time between a failure and full service restoration. Expressed in hours, minutes, or seconds.
the maximum acceptable amount of data loss, measured as a point in time. An RPO of 1 hour means you can tolerate losing at most the last hour of data.
S3 backups, AWS Backup, RDS snapshots
Minimal DR resources kept warm; AMIs pre-baked
Scaled-down replica in second region, sync data
DynamoDB Global Tables, Aurora Global Database, Route 53 latency routing

Amazon S3 Standard achieves 99.999999999% object durability — eleven nines — by storing each object redundantly across a minimum of three AZs within a region. This means that even if AWS were to lose an entire AZ simultaneously, every object would survive intact in the other AZs. To put this in perspective: at 11 nines of durability, storing 10 million objects for 10,000 years would expect to lose one object.
S3 performs checksums on every read and write and automatically repairs any detected corruption by copying the intact replica
S3 Versioning protects against accidental deletion or overwrite — the previous version is retained
S3 Cross-Region Replication (CRR) copies objects to a second region for geographic redundancy and compliance requirements
Object Lock (WORM mode) prevents any process — including account administrators — from deleting objects during a retention period, satisfying regulatory data-protection requirements
Chaos Engineering with AWS Fault Injection Simulator

A system is only as reliable as its untested assumptions. AWS Fault Injection Simulator (FIS) lets you run controlled failure experiments in production or pre-production environments — deliberately terminating EC2 instances, injecting CPU pressure, throttling API calls, or introducing network latency to validate that your architecture recovers as designed.

You cannot improve what you cannot measure. Reliability engineering requires observability: the ability to understand the internal state of your system from the outputs it produces. AWS provides a native observability stack:
Alarm on error rate, latency p99, CPU saturation
Log Insights to query failure patterns across fleets
Identify which service in a call chain is degraded
Route EC2 state-change events to runbook automation
Proactively notify when AWS-side issues affect your resources

Higher availability targets cost more. Deploying across two AZs roughly doubles instance, storage, and data-transfer costs for stateful components. A third AZ adds further cost but provides protection against the unlikely scenario where two AZs are simultaneously impaired. Multi-region active-active architectures can triple or quadruple the infrastructure bill while achieving sub-second RTO.
Dev, test, batch workloads with no SLA
Most production web and API workloads

Not every workload needs five nines. Over-engineering reliability wastes money. The right question is: what is the cost of one hour of downtime for this specific system? If that cost is lower than the price of the additional redundancy, the simpler architecture is correct.
AWS encodes reliability best practices in the Well-Architected Framework's Reliability Pillar. Its five design principles are:
use health checks and automation to detect and remediate failures without human intervention
use chaos engineering to validate that failover paths work — do not wait for real failures to discover they do not
Scale horizontally to reduce the impact of individual component failure:
replace one large resource with multiple smaller resources so any single failure has smaller blast radius
use Auto Scaling to provision enough capacity automatically; over-provisioning masks reliability problems, under-provisioning creates them
infrastructure changes applied through CI/CD pipelines are more reliable than manual changes; use CloudFormation or CDK for reproducible deployments

AWS publishes SLAs for its managed services. If AWS falls below the committed availability target, customers receive service credits. Understanding AWS SLAs helps you design architectures that can realistically hit your own SLA commitments to end customers — your availability is bounded by the product of the SLAs of every service in the critical path.
Availability SLA; durability is 11 nines (separate guarantee)
To calculate your application's realistic ceiling: if the critical path goes EC2 → RDS → S3, the combined availability is approximately
— about 99.84%. To exceed this, you need redundancy at each layer so that no single service failure takes down the whole application.
Designing for Resilience: Patterns and Anti-patterns
Building reliable systems on AWS requires deliberate design choices. The following patterns address the most common reliability gaps seen in production workloads:

A circuit breaker monitors calls to a downstream service. When the failure rate exceeds a threshold, the circuit 'opens' and all subsequent calls are rejected immediately (returning a fallback response) rather than waiting for a timeout. This prevents a slow or failed dependency from exhausting the calling service's thread pool and causing a cascading failure across the entire system.
normal operation — requests flow through and failures are counted
circuit has tripped — requests fail fast for a cooldown period without hitting the downstream
after the cooldown, one probe request is let through; if it succeeds the circuit closes, if not it stays open
Timeouts and Retries with Exponential Backoff

Every remote call must have a timeout. Unbounded waits turn a slow dependency into a stuck thread and eventually an OOM crash. When retrying transient failures, use exponential backoff with jitter: each retry waits twice as long as the previous one, and a random jitter prevents all retrying clients from hammering the recovering service at the same instant.

Named after a ship's watertight compartments, the bulkhead pattern isolates different workloads so a failure in one cannot consume resources needed by the others. On AWS this translates to running different workload tiers in separate Auto Scaling groups with independent CPU and connection pool limits, or dedicating separate Lambda concurrency reservations to critical vs background tasks.

# Reserve concurrency for a critical payment function
# so a burst in a background Lambda cannot starve it
aws lambda put-function-concurrency \
  --function-name process-payment \
  --reserved-concurrent-executions 200

# Throttle the background job to leave headroom
aws lambda put-function-concurrency \
  --function-name generate-report \
  --reserved-concurrent-executions 20
Recognising what to avoid is as important as knowing the positive patterns:
AZ outage = hours of downtime while snapshot is restored
Enable RDS Multi-AZ or use Aurora (3 AZ by default)
Recovered instance gets a new IP; all callers break
Use DNS names (ALB endpoint, RDS endpoint, Route 53) for all inter-service calls
One slow service causes timeout cascade through every caller
Use SQS or EventBridge to decouple; downstream processes at its own pace
Failover procedure exists on paper but has never been exercised; it fails during an actual incident
Run regular FIS experiments; include failover in runbook tests
Instance replacement or AZ failover loses user sessions
Externalise sessions to ElastiCache (Redis) or DynamoDB
Reliability for Managed Services vs Self-managed Components

A significant advantage of AWS managed services is the transfer of operational reliability burden. When you run Kafka on EC2 you own broker replacement, replication lag monitoring, and rolling upgrades. When you use Amazon MSK (Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka), AWS handles broker replacement in response to hardware failure — you configure replication factor and retention, AWS handles the physical redundancy.
Automated Multi-AZ failover, patch scheduling, backups
Serverless, redundant storage across AZs, at-least-once delivery
Automatic replica promotion on primary failure

Serverless architectures shift most infrastructure reliability concerns to AWS. Lambda functions execute on fully managed infrastructure that automatically handles underlying hardware failures. However, application-level reliability still requires careful design:
configure a DLQ on asynchronous Lambda invocations so that events that fail after retries are not silently dropped — they are captured in SQS or SNS for investigation and reprocessing
Lambda may retry a function on failure, meaning the same event can be processed more than once — design handlers to be idempotent so duplicate processing produces the same result
without reserved concurrency, a burst in one function can exhaust the regional concurrency quota and starve other critical functions
for multi-step workflows, AWS Step Functions persists state between steps so a failure at step 4 can retry from step 4 rather than restarting the entire workflow

Every incident is an opportunity to raise the reliability baseline. A well-run post-incident review (sometimes called a blameless post-mortem) identifies contributing factors without assigning personal blame, then produces concrete action items — typically in the form of runbook updates, new monitoring alarms, or architectural changes.
use CloudTrail API logs, CloudWatch log timestamps, and X-Ray traces to build an accurate timeline of what changed and when
drill past symptoms to root causes — 'the service went down' is not a root cause; 'an AZ failover was not rehearsed and the runbook step was wrong' is
Action items with owners and due dates:
unowned items are never completed — assign every action to a named person with a deadline
if the incident was discovered by a customer rather than an alarm, that gap must be closed before the next occurrence

AWS provides AWS Systems Manager OpsCenter to centralise operational items and aggregated CloudWatch metrics, making it easier to correlate alarms and triage incidents without switching between multiple consoles:

# Create a CloudWatch alarm that fires a Systems Manager OpsItem automatically
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name high-5xx-rate \
  --namespace AWS/ApplicationELB \
  --metric-name HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count \
  --dimensions Name=LoadBalancer,Value=app/my-alb/abc123 \
  --period 60 \
  --evaluation-periods 3 \
  --threshold 50 \
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold \
  --statistic Sum \
  --alarm-actions arn:aws:ssm:us-east-1:123456789012:opsitem:severity/2#CATEGORY=Availability \
  --treat-missing-data notBreaching
Availability is measured in nines; each additional nine reduces allowable downtime by a factor of ten and requires architectural investment to achieve
MTBF and MTTR are the operational levers: cloud automation improves both by replacing failed hardware faster and recovering services automatically without human intervention
AWS Availability Zones are the primary resilience building block — distributing instances, databases, and caches across AZs within a region is the first step toward 99.99% availability
RTO and RPO define what your system promises when things go wrong; choose the DR strategy (backup-restore, pilot light, warm standby, active-active) that meets your targets at acceptable cost
Test your reliability assumptions with chaos engineering (AWS FIS) before production failures reveal them; automated recovery only works if it has been validated end-to-end
